42: lithon hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomountes houtos egeneto eis kephalen gonias — the stone which the builders rejected, this one became the head of the corner — apedokimasan (rejected after examination, found to be unfit) is the technical term for the stone-mason's rejection of a stone after inspection. Kephalen gonias (head of the corner) denotes either the capstone (keystone completing an arch) or the cornerstone (the principal foundation stone that sets the line of the building). Both readings apply Christologically: the rejected Jesus becomes the load-bearing stone of the new community. The citation is Psalms 118:22-23 LXX.
1: ēggisan eis Hierosolyma — drew near to Jerusalem — Eggizō (to draw near) marks the beginning of the Passion Week narrative. Bethphage (Bēthphagē = house of unripe figs in Aramaic) is on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives; the phrase tote Iēsous apesteilen (then Jesus sent) opens the deliberate arrangement of the entry.
2: onon dedemenen kai pōlon met' autēs — a donkey tied and a colt with her — Matthew uniquely has two animals (cf. Mark/Luke: one). The dedemenen (perfect passive participle: having been tied) and lysantes (loosing, aorist) echo a prophetic staging. The ho kyrios autōn chreian echei (the Lord has need of them) phrase uses chreian echei = has need, an expression of genuine requirement.
3: ho kyrios autōn chreian echei — the Lord has need of them — The absolute use of ho kyrios (the Lord) without further specification echoes LXX divine-address usage; the disciples apply it directly to Jesus. Euthys de apostellei autous (and immediately he will send them) — the future apostellei implies a prearranged signal.
4: hina plērōthē to rhēthen dia tou prophētou — in order that what was spoken through the prophet might be fulfilled — Hina plērōthē + aorist passive subjunctive is Matthew’s standard fulfillment formula. The citation combines Isaiah 62:11 (LXX) and Zechariah 9:9, both messianic texts; Matthew’s conflation of sources is deliberate editorial work.
5: idou ho basileus sou erchetai soi prays — behold your king comes to you meek — From Zechariah 9:9 LXX. Prays (meek, gentle) recurs from Matthew 11:29 (Jesus’ self-description) and Matthew 5:5 (Beatitudes). Epibebēkōs epi onon kai epi pōlon (mounted on a donkey and on a colt) — the Hebrew parallelism of Zechariah 9:9 (synonymous: one animal) is taken as two animals by Matthew’s literal reading.
6: poiēsantes kathōs synetaxen autois ho Iēsous — having done as Jesus directed them — Syntassō (to arrange, direct, command) aorist; the disciples’ obedience is exact and immediate. The aorist participle poiēsantes precedes the main verb, emphasizing compliance before arrival.
7: epethēkan ep' autōn ta himatia — they put the garments on them — Draping garments over the animals and spreading them on the road echoes the royal acclamation of Jehu in 2 Kings 9:13 (LXX: elabon hekastos to himation autou kai ethēkan). Ekathisen epanō autōn (he sat upon them) — ambiguous plural; likely refers to the garments, not both animals.
8: ho de pleistos ochlos estrōsan — the very large crowd spread — Pleistos is the superlative of polys: the largest/very great crowd. Estrōsan (spread, strewed) their garments on the road; others cut kladous apo tōn dendrōn (branches from the trees) — not specifically palm branches as in John 12:13; Matthew’s kladoi = leafy branches generically.
9: hōsanna tō huiō Dauid — Hosanna to the Son of David — Hōsanna is the Greek transliteration of Heb. hōšîaʿ-nāʾ (save, please! / O save!) from Psalms 118:25-26. By Jesus’ day it had become an acclamation of royal welcome, having lost its strict imperative force. Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati kyriou quotes Psalms 118:26 LXX verbatim; ho erchomenos (the coming one) is a recognized Messianic designation (cf. Matthew 11:3).
10: eseisthē pasa hē polis — all the city was shaken — Seiō (to shake, quake) is used for earthquake (Matthew 27:51; Matthew 28:4); its application to the city’s reaction creates an eschatological resonance. Tis estin houtos (who is this?) — the city’s question mirrors the sea-stilling disciples’ question (Matthew 8:27); Jerusalem does not recognize its king.
11: houtos estin ho prophētēs Iēsous ho apo Nazareth tēs Galilaias — this is the prophet Jesus from Nazareth of Galilee — The crowd identifies Jesus as ho prophētēs (the prophet), recalling the eschatological prophet of Deuteronomy 18:15-18. The full geographical designation (ho apo Nazareth tēs Galilaias) marks regional identity; Jerusalem crowds may intend a slight by the Galilean label.
12: exebalen pantas tous pōlountas kai agorazontas — he drove out all those selling and buying — Ekballō (to throw out, expel) is the exorcism verb; its use here frames the temple cleansing as analogous to casting out an unclean presence. Katestrepsen tas trapezas (overturned the tables) — katastrephō = to turn over completely, overturn.
13: ho oikos mou oikos proseuchēs klēthēsetai — my house shall be called a house of prayer — Quoting Isaiah 56:7 LXX. Klēthēsetai (shall be called) future passive = this is its established divine designation. Hymeis de auton poieite spēlaion lēstōn (but you are making it a den of robbers) — quotes Jeremiah 7:11 LXX. Spēlaion (cave, den) + lēstōn (robbers, bandits — Josephus uses lēstēs for violent insurgents) is a jarring contrast to oikos proseuchēs.
14: proseēlthan autō typhloi kai chōloi — the blind and the lame came to him — The temple healing follows immediately on the cleansing; the restored temple becomes a place of healing. Typhloi (blind) and chōloi (lame) are those excluded from the temple precincts in Leviticus 21:18 and 2 Samuel 5:8; Jesus reverses the exclusion.
15: ta thaumasia ha epoiēsen — the wonderful things he did — Thaumasia (wonderful, marvelous things) is the LXX word for God’s mighty acts (Psalms 71:17 LXX). The chief priests’ and scribes’ indignation at hōsanna tō huiō Dauid from children reveals their opposition to the Davidic Messianic claim.
16: ek stomatos nēpiōn kai thēlazontōn katērtisō ainon — from the mouth of infants and nursing ones you have prepared praise — Quoting Psalms 8:3 LXX (MT: ʿoz = strength/power; LXX: ainon = praise — Matthew follows LXX). Jesus applies this to the children’s cry; the infant praise vindicates the legitimacy of the acclamation the authorities reject.
17: exelthen exō tēs poleōs eis Bēthanian — he went out of the city to Bethany — Jesus’ nightly withdrawal to Bethany (cf. Matthew 21:18) is a structural feature of Passion Week; he does not lodge in Jerusalem. Ēulisthē ekei (spent the night there) — aulizomai = to spend the night outdoors or in a courtyard, appropriate to Passover practices.
18: prōï de epanagōn eis tēn polin epeinase — returning early to the city he was hungry — Prōï (early) and epanagōn (returning, aorist participle) frame the fig tree episode on the morning after the entry. Epeinase (was hungry) — the aorist describes a state; Matthew’s Jesus experiences genuine physical hunger, affirming full humanity.
19: ouden heuron en autē ei mē phylla monon — he found nothing in it except leaves only — Ei mē phylla monon (except leaves only) — the monon (only) is emphatic. Fig trees in Palestine develop fruit buds before full foliage; leaves without any fruit signal abnormality. Exēranthē parachrēma hē sykē (the fig tree withered immediately) — parachrēma = immediately, at once; Matthew compresses Mark’s two-day sequence into one.
20: pōs parachrēma exēranthē hē sykē — how did the fig tree wither immediately? — The disciples’ amazement focuses on the instantaneous effect (parachrēma). Exēranthē (aorist passive of xērainō = to dry up, wither) is the same verb used for the withering of seed sown on rocky ground (Matthew 13:6).
21: ean echēte pistin kai mē diakrthēte — if you have faith and do not doubt — Diakrinō in the passive or middle = to doubt, be divided (in judgment); literally to separate, discriminate. Ou monon to tēs sykēs poiēsete (not only the fig tree thing will you do) — the to tēs sykēs (the fig-tree thing) is a demonstrative articular phrase referring to the specific act. Tō̧ orei toutō̧ (to this mountain) = presumably the Mount of Olives.
22: panta hosa an aitēsēte en tē proseuchē pisteuontes lēmpsesthe — all things whatever you ask in prayer believing you will receive — Pisteuontes (present active participle: believing) is the condition; the promise is governed by faith-in-prayer, not raw petition. Lēmpsesthe future indicative = you will receive — the confidence of promise, not mere possibility.
23: en poiā exousiā tauta poieis — by what authority do you do these things? — Exousia (authority, from exesti = it is permitted) was the central question in Jewish religious politics: who had authorized teaching, healing, and temple action? Kai tis soi edōken tēn exousian tautēn (and who gave you this authority?) — both the basis and source of authority are challenged.
24: erōtēsō hymas kagō logon hena — I will ask you also one word — Jesus’ counter-question (logon hena = one question/matter) employs the rabbinic debate technique of silencing an adversary with a counter-dilemma. Hon ean eipēte moi kagō hymin erō (which if you tell me I will also tell you) — the conditional is a formal debate commitment.
25: to baptisma to Iōannou pothen ēn — the baptism of John, from where was it? — Ex ouranou ē ex anthrōpōn (from heaven or from humans) — the binary forces recognition: divine authorization or human invention. Ex ouranou is the circumlocution for divine origin common in Jewish speech to avoid the divine name.
26: pantes gar hōs prophētēn echousin ton Iōannēn — for all hold John as a prophet — The authorities’ reasoning is transparent: political calculation rather than theological judgment. Phoboumenoi ton ochlon (fearing the crowd) governs their answer; this reveals that their authority rests on crowd management, not divine truth.
27: oude egō legō hymin en poiā exousiā tauta poiō — neither do I tell you by what authority I do these things — The refusal is not evasion but a declaration: their disqualification from receiving the answer. Ouk oidamen (we do not know) was dishonest — they had deliberated the answer; their own dishonesty disqualifies them.
28: anthrōpos eichen tekna dyo — a man had two children — The parable of the two sons sets up a direct application to the authorities and toll collectors. Teknon (child) rather than huios (son) emphasizes the paternal relationship. Hypage sēmeron ergazou en tō̧ ampelōni (go today, work in the vineyard) — the sēmeron (today) stresses urgency.
29: ho de apokritheis eipen ou thelō — he answered and said: I do not wish to — The direct refusal ou thelō (I do not wish) is then reversed: hysteron metamelētheis apēlthen (later having changed his mind he went). Metamélomai (to regret, have a change of feeling) differs from metanoeō (repentance from sin); it is emotional reversal leading to action.
30: ho de apokritheis eipen egō kyrie — he answered and said: I, lord — The second son’s instant formal compliance (egō kyrie = I, sir/lord) and then failure to act exposes the hypocrisy. Ouk apēlthen (did not go) — the aorist negation is definitive; he never went.
31: hoi telōnai kai hai pornai proagousin hymas eis tēn basileian — the tax collectors and prostitutes go before you into the kingdom — Proagousin (lead the way, go before) is a shocking reversal: those considered most impure enter first. Telōnai (toll collectors) and pornai (prostitutes) represent religious and moral outsiders respectively — both responded to John’s call.
32: ēlthen gar Iōannēs pros hymas en hodō dikaiosynēs — for John came to you in the way of righteousness — Hodos dikaiosynēs (way/path of righteousness) is a Hebrew idiom (derek ṣedeq) for righteous conduct and teaching. Ou metemelēthēte hysteron (you did not afterwards change your mind) — the same verb metamélomai from Matthew 21:29; the authorities lack even the emotional reversal the first son displayed.
33: allēn parabolēn akousate — hear another parable — The Wicked Tenants parable draws on Isaiah 5:1-7 (Song of the Vineyard); phragmon (fence/hedge), lēnon (winepress), pyrgon (tower) all appear in Isaiah’s vineyard description, making the allusion deliberate and clear to any biblically literate hearer.
34: apesteilen tous doulous autou pros tous geōrgous — he sent his servants to the tenants — Geōrgos (farmer, vine-dresser, tenant) = one who works the earth. Labein tous karpous autou (to receive his fruits) — the owner’s expectation is a share of the produce, the standard lease arrangement. The servants represent the prophets.
35: labontes hoi geōrgoi tous doulous autou — the tenants having seized his servants — Three fates: edeiran (flogged/beat), apektenan (killed), elithobolēsan (stoned) — the escalating violence against prophets mirrors Israel’s historical rejection of prophets (cf. Nehemiah 9:26; Hebrews 11:36-37).
36: apesteilen allous doulous pleionas tōn prōtōn — he sent other servants more than the first — Pleionas (more in number) — the second delegation is larger; the same treatment ensues. Matthew’s two waves of servants (vs. Mark’s individual sequence) emphasizes the pattern of rejection.
37: hysteron de apesteilen pros autous ton huion autou — last of all he sent his son to them — Hysteron (finally, last of all) marks the climax. Entrapēsontai (they will respect, be shamed before) uses entrepō = to turn toward with respect; the owner’s reasoning is generous but fatally optimistic — the dramatic irony is complete.
38: deute apokteinōmen auton kai schōmen tēn klēronomian autou — come let us kill him and possess his inheritance — Deute (come!) is an imperative of invitation/incitement. Klēronomia (inheritance) = the estate that would pass to the heir; killing the heir before the owner’s death was a known legal strategy in some Roman contexts. The tenants’ logic reveals deliberate murderous calculation.
39: ekballon exō tou ampelōnos apekteinan — having thrown him outside the vineyard they killed him — The sequence ekballon exō (throwing outside) before killing mirrors Jesus’ own fate: he is taken outside Jerusalem (exō tēs pylēs, Hebrews 13:12) before crucifixion. Matthew places this in the same order as the historical Passion.
40: hotan oun elthē ho kyrios tou ampelōnos — when therefore the lord of the vineyard comes — Jesus turns the parable into a question, forcing the authorities to pronounce their own judgment. Tí poiēsei toîs geōrgois ekeinois (what will he do to those tenants?) — the future deliberative implicates them as judges of their own case.
41: kakous kakōs apolesei autous — evildoers he will utterly destroy — The figure kakous kakōs is a Greek wordplay (kakos/kakōs: evil ones evilly) constituting a paronomasia: evil men will meet an evil end. Ekdōsetai ton ampelōna allois geōrgois (he will lease the vineyard to other tenants) — the authorities unwittingly pronounce the transfer of the covenant community to others.
43: arthēsetai aph' hymōn hē basileia tou theou — the kingdom of God will be taken from you — Arthēsetai (future passive of airō = to take up, remove) is a decisive eschatological statement. Kai dothēsetai ethnei poiounti tous karpous autēs (and given to a nation producing its fruits) — ethnos here likely refers not to Gentiles specifically but to the reconstituted people of God who bear kingdom fruit.
44: ho pesōn epi ton lithon touton synthlasthēsetai — the one falling on this stone will be broken to pieces — Synthlaomai (to shatter, crush to pieces) and likmēsei auton (it will grind him to powder) — likmáō = to winnow, scatter; LXX uses it for divine judgment-scattering. The two images (fall on stone = shattered; stone falls on you = pulverized) both end in destruction; the stone is inescapable in either direction.
45: egnōsan hoti peri autōn legei — they knew that he was speaking about them — Egnōsan (aorist of ginōskō) — the recognition is complete; the application of the parable to themselves is understood. The perception drives their desire to seize him rather than repentance.
46: kai zētountes auton kratēsai ephobēthēsan tous ochlous — seeking to seize him they feared the crowds — Kratēsai (to seize, arrest) aorist infinitive; ephobēthēsan (feared) governed by crowd support for Jesus. Hoti eis prophētēn auton eichon (because they held him as a prophet) — the same identification as Matthew 21:11; the popular perception protects Jesus from immediate arrest.
